macro_events.event_summary: With a criminal probe against him concluded, Fed Chair Powell faces a decision on whether to stay in his role or step down, creating uncertainty about Fed leadership continuity.
primary_drivers: Fed leadership uncertainty affecting rate path expectations
Potential shift toward more dovish successor if Powell departs
headlines: Will he stay or will he go? With criminal probe over, Fed Chair Powell faces big decision

macro_events.event_summary: Baker Hughes says the Strait of Hormuz — which carries ~20% of global oil and ~20% of LNG trade — may not fully reopen until 2H 2026, implying an extended disruption to Middle East energy exports.
primary_drivers: Extended Middle East oil/LNG supply disruption → sustained higher crude and LNG prices
Shift in LNG demand toward non-Hormuz suppliers (US Gulf Coast exporters)
Tanker/shipping rate spikes and rerouting around the Cape
Risk premium and defense spending tailwind

beneficiaries.rationale: Coterra is a pure-play US onshore oil & gas producer (Permian/Anadarko) with no Hormuz exposure; extended Brent risk premium directly expands realized pricing and FCF on unhedged production.
Diamondback is a Permian pure-play with high oil cut and low break-even; sustained $90+ Brent for 12+ months drives material FCF upside and variable dividend payouts.

Cheniere is the largest US LNG exporter (Sabine Pass, Corpus Christi); Hormuz closure forces European/Asian buyers toward US Gulf cargoes, tightening global LNG and lifting spot margins on uncontracted volumes.
Venture Global operates Calcasieu Pass and Plaquemines LNG with significant spot-market exposure; Hormuz outage through 2026 directly tightens Atlantic LNG and boosts cargo netbacks.
Flex LNG owns a fleet of modern LNG carriers on variable-rate charters; Hormuz rerouting increases ton-mile demand and spot charter rates materially.
Scorpio Tankers (product tankers) benefits from crude/product rerouting around the Cape of Good Hope, extending voyage lengths and tightening tanker supply — direct driver of day rates.
International Seaways operates crude and product tankers; extended Hormuz closure forces longer-haul routing (Cape) which structurally lifts VLCC/Suezmax rates for the duration.
L3Harris provides Gulf-region ISR, maritime surveillance and countermine systems; prolonged Hormuz conflict sustains supplemental defense appropriations and munitions reorders.

losers.rationale: Delta has the least-hedged jet fuel book among US majors; sustained crude premium through 2026 directly compresses unit margins as fuel is ~25-30% of opex.
United has heavy international long-haul exposure (premium transatlantic/Pacific routes) with no fuel hedging; extended oil premium plus potential Gulf airspace reroutes compress margins.
Southwest's low-cost domestic model is highly fuel-sensitive; unlike international peers it cannot offset via premium cabin mix, so EPS cuts on sustained Brent premium are direct.
Carnival's bunker fuel is a major cost line and Middle East/Red Sea itineraries are already disrupted; extended Hormuz closure prolongs rerouting costs and fuel headwinds.
Dow is heavily exposed to naphtha/ethane feedstock and global petrochemical spreads; extended energy shock compresses chemical margins as price pass-through lags in a soft demand environment.
Counterintuitive loser at the margin: ExxonMobil's large Qatar LNG JV stake and Gulf upstream assets (UAE, Iraq) face stranded production; offsets some benefit from higher Brent on non-Gulf barrels.

headlines: Strait of Hormuz may not fully reopen until second half of 2026, Baker Hughes says

macro_events.event_summary: Crude oil prices are expected to remain structurally elevated post-Iran war, with no near-term return to pre-conflict levels due to persistent geopolitical risk premium and supply disruption.
primary_drivers: Sustained geopolitical risk premium embedded in crude
Middle East supply disruption / sanctions on Iranian barrels tightening global supply
Higher-for-longer oil prices boost upstream cash flows and hurt fuel-cost-sensitive businesses

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with ~60% upstream earnings mix; every $10/bbl sustained uplift adds multi-billion to annual CFO. Direct leverage to higher realized prices with no Iran exposure.
Pure-play E&P with high oil beta (~70% liquids), no downstream drag. Sustained Brent premium flows directly to FCF and buyback capacity.
Permian pure-play with low breakevens (~$40); every dollar above that is near-pure margin. Structurally higher strip re-rates NAV.
High oil beta, levered balance sheet means higher crude disproportionately accelerates debt paydown and equity value. Buffett-backed visibility.
Global oilfield services leader; sustained high prices drive international upstream capex cycle, particularly Middle East NOC spending which is SLB's strongest geography.
Midstream gatherer/processor with volume leverage to Permian activity; sustained crude above $75 keeps rig counts and throughput elevated.

losers.rationale: Jet fuel is ~25-30% of operating costs and Southwest has historically unhedged or lightly hedged; sustained higher crude directly compresses unit margins with limited pricing power in domestic leisure.
Fuel is largest non-labor cost; owns its own refinery (Monroe) providing partial hedge but net still a large consumer. Higher structural jet crack spreads pressure EPS.
Fuel cost exposure plus international route mix sensitive to demand destruction if pump prices stay elevated into summer travel season.
Ground and air fleet fuel costs are material; surcharge lag and contractual pricing means margin compression before pass-through fully catches up.
Bunker fuel is major cost line for cruise operators; sustained crude ~$80+ pressures already-rebuilding margins and can't be fully surcharged on booked cruises.
Oil-linked feedstocks (naphtha) raise input costs for commodity chemicals; weak pricing power in current chemical cycle means margin squeeze.

headlines: Why crude prices won’t fall back to levels seen before the Iran war anytime soon
